coredata.dc:description: The levels of metabolites in the human body provide significant clues for the diagnosis, staging, and therapy planning of diseases. Recently, fluorescence-based metabolite detection methods have become popular thanks to their important advantages such as using less sample, not requiring prior preparation, and responding quickly. In this regard, quantum dots (QDs) are unique materials in metabolite detection studies due to their well-known fluorescence-emitting structures, high luminescence, tunable size, and high absorption cross-section ratio. In this experiment, we synthesized the mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped CdSeTe-alloy QDs and investigated their metabolite detection performance using vitamin C (VitC), folic acid (FA) and glucose (Glu). Under the optimized conditions (pH, QD concentration and incubation times), the quenching fluorescent responses were obtained from metabolite-added CdSeTe QD solution which ascribed to the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The linearity ranges and limit of detection (LOD) values for VitC, FA and Glu were determined. The presented method has also been applied to quantify the amount of metabolites in human blood serum samples in the linear ranges of 10–20 µg/mL for CVit, 7.5–20 ng/mL for FA, and 0.25–1.5 mg/mL for glucose, with an accuracy of over 95 %. This sensor system has also shown a noteworthy advantage regarding sensitive, selective, reliable and cost-effective metabolite detection technique.

head.ce:title: CdSeTe quantum dot based fluorescence sensor systems for detection of vitamin C, folic acid and glucose

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The levels of metabolites in the human body provide significant clues for the diagnosis, staging, and therapy planning of diseases. Recently, fluorescence-based metabolite detection methods have become popular thanks to their important advantages such as using less sample, not requiring prior preparation, and responding quickly. In this regard, quantum dots (QDs) are unique materials in metabolite detection studies due to their well-known fluorescence-emitting structures, high luminescence, tunable size, and high absorption cross-section ratio. In this experiment, we synthesized the mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped CdSeTe-alloy QDs and investigated their metabolite detection performance using vitamin C (VitC), folic acid (FA) and glucose (Glu). Under the optimized conditions (pH, QD concentration and incubation times), the quenching fluorescent responses were obtained from metabolite-added CdSeTe QD solution which ascribed to the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The linearity ranges and limit of detection (LOD) values for VitC, FA and Glu were determined. The presented method has also been applied to quantify the amount of metabolites in human blood serum samples in the linear ranges of 10–20 µg/mL for CVit, 7.5–20 ng/mL for FA, and 0.25–1.5 mg/mL for glucose, with an accuracy of over 95 %. This sensor system has also shown a noteworthy advantage regarding sensitive, selective, reliable and cost-effective metabolite detection technique.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor QDs stand out for their distinctive optical and electronic properties such as narrow emission spectrum, size-dependent emission characteristics and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) over the past twenty years. These materials have shown great promise in numerous application areas, including, light-emitting diodes [1–5], solar cells [6–10], tumour cell imaging and destruction [11–17], biomedical imaging [18–23] and biological metabolite detection [24–28]. Monitoring human metabolism is essential for developing an effective treatment plan that is unique to each patient. In this regard, simple and quick procedures for the quantitative detection of metabolites in biological media are crucial for diagnosis and medical care [29]. Although, currently, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [30–35], colorimetry [36,37], electrochemiluminescence (EC) [38–40], molecular imprinting [41–43] and electrochemistry [44–46] are preferred as metabolite assay techniques, they have generally some negative aspects such as low selectivity, long analysis time, wasting sample and difficult pretreatment [47]. On the other hand, their applications occasionally revealed excessive costs. Furthermore, the majority of these techniques rely on manual sample handling and measurement and employ non-automatic approaches. Also, the most of currently used techniques are renowned for their expensive equipment needs, time-consuming processes, and labor-intensive methodologies; for example, issues with selectivity and sensitivity hamper electro-analytical techniques for detecting metabolites. The traditional HPLC technique necessitates a laborious sample preparation process to prevent matrix interference and eliminate any potentially disruptive compounds from the matrix [48]. Poor sensitivity is the main flaw in the EC approach, which restricts its use to detecting traces of metabolites in particular biological samples [49]. Therefore, it is important to develop more accurate, selective, economical and user-friendly detection technologies. At this point, the fluorescence method for metabolite detection [28,50–52] rises due to its advantages such as sensitivity, easy applicability, low cost and rapid response [51] over the common techniques. In addition, these advantages make fluorescence detection systems suitable for on-site applications wherein real-time analysis, high sensitivity, compactness, and user-friendliness are key parameters. Fluorescence-based optical sensors typically use a single fluorescence signal to identify their targets. Analytes can be identified and quantified with great ease because of the visualization impact of the fluorescence technique [53]. The peak wavelength or intensity of the fluorescence will change, resulting in a color shift, when a fluorescent probe detects varying analyte concentrations. Numerous fluorescence sensors have been reported in the literature for the determination of pH [54,55], cations [56–58], vapours [59], anions [60], explosives [61–63] and small molecules [57,64,65]. However, despite various advantages of fluorescence sensors mentioned above, they also can suffer from challenges including, application scope and slow detection and design complexity [66]. Although the common fluorescence sensors are well known for their sensitivity, they sometimes might give false-positive results because of their lack of specificity [67].

In this regard, QDs have the superiority over the common fluorescence methods to detect metabolites. Due to emitting bright and steady fluorescence, the QD-based method is highly sensitive such that it is capable of detecting trace quantities of metabolites. QDs are also capable of detecting metabolites in a wide dynamic range (ngr to µgr) due to their size-dependent optical properties [68]. Furthermore, QDs are relatively stable in several conditions, including pH levels [69], light exposure [70] and oxidative environment [71]. This feature is crucial for precise and stable metabolite detection. Likewise, QDs offer easy functionalization so that they easily interact with the targeted metabolite in a detection process [72]. Overall, QDs’ application in detecting metabolites has great potential for developing the knowledge of biological mechanisms.

FA is a water-soluble B-type vitamin and it can only obtained from diet [68] of common nutrients such as meat, vegetables, fruits and grains [73]. Although FA is necessary for red blood cell creation, DNA synthesis and repair in the body, its deficiency causes numerous diseases. The excessive FA levels also cause serious problems as it inhibits zinc and vitamin B12 absorption [74]. It is essential to develop straightforward, sensitive, and selective methods for detecting folic acid in biological systems because FA is required for the healthy operation of numerous physiological processes in humans. An FA level of 3–6 ng/mL is considered to be standard in healthy human blood [75]. VitC known as ascorbic acid is another important metabolite that plays a crucial role in the human body. Its deficiency causes the diseases of anemia, gingivitis and scurvy, while its excess in the body can lead to kidney disorders. It functions as a cofactor for a range of gene-regulating and biosynthetic enzymes and supports immunological defence [76]. VitC also treats systemic and respiratory infections [77]. The recommended amount of VitC in the blood serum of a healthy person is 0.6–2.0 mg/dL[78]. On the other hand, Glu is a main energy source for the body and crucial for carbohydrate metabolism, aerobic and anaerobic respiration, photosynthesis, growth and reproduction in most living things. It acts as a metabolic bridge during the creation of other complex compounds. Low blood sugar levels can result in elevated cardiovascular risk, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and hypertension [79]. A Glu concentration range of 70–100 mg/dL in body fluids is considered to be normal in adults [80]. Overall, monitoring the VitC, FA and Glu levels is crucial for the diagnosis and control of diseases [81–83].

In metabolite detection studies carried out with QDs in biological environments, water-soluble QDs are preferred so that metabolite-QD interactions can occur efficiently. Because of QDs' special qualities in aqueous media and the biological applications, they can serve, there is now a great deal of research being done on using QD-based biosensors. Water-soluble QDs have been applied to the construction of biosensors as well as the identification of significant biological substances in the human system. In addition, the synthesis of water-soluble QDs is less expensive, less toxic, simpler and biocompatible [84]. Various acids such as thioglycolic acid (TGA) [85], glutathione (GSH) [86], mercaptoacetic acid (MAA) [87] or mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) [88] are commonly used for the water-based synthesis of QDs. CdSeTe ternary QDs are well known for their high stability, easy fabrication, high PLQY, versatility and water solubility and have not been tested for metabolite detection systems to the best of Author’s knowledge. In this study, a novel MPA-capped CdSeTe QD-based fluorescence sensor was developed for the detection of VitC, FA and Glu which are selected as models in this paper. These substances are found naturally in fruits and vegetables, and employed as a necessary food additive, and function as an antioxidant in variety of chemical and biological systems. Therefore, it is crucial to develop straightforward, sensitive, and selective methods for determining these analytes. In this study, under the optimized QD concentration, pH and incubation time conditions, VitC, FA and glucose amounts were detected by comparing quenched the fluorescence intensity of CdSeTe QDs. The proposed metabolite detection method was also applied to detect VitC, FA and Glu in real blood serum samples.

Ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.9 %), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, >98.0 %), folic acid (>97.0 %), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, >99.99 %), tellurium powder (Te, >99.8 %), selenium powder (Se, >99.99 %), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, >99.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, >98.0 %), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, >98 %), sodium sulfide nanohydrate (Na2S·9H2O, >99.99 %), and glucose oxidase (>95.0 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. While hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37.0 %) was obtained from ISOLAB, D(+)-Glucose (>99.0 %) was obtained from AFG Bioscience. Vitamin C (injection) was bought from the local pharmacy. All chemicals were used as of analytical grade and double distilled water (ddw) was used in all experiments.

UV–Vis and fluorescence measurements were conducted using Hach DR3900 and Horiba FluoroMax, respectively. Raigaku Smartlab XRD with CuKα radiation of 1.5406 Å diffractometer was used to record X-ray diffraction patterns. Shimadzu IRPrestige-21 and Edinburg Instruments FLS1000 spectrometer were used to acquire Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer (FTIR) and PLQY measurements. Finally, FEI TALOS F200S TEM microscopy operating at 200 kV was employed to record the HR-TEM measurements.

CdSeTe QDs were synthesized according to the previous reports with slight modifications [89–91]. Firstly, for Cd+2 precursor solution, 0.4 mmol CdCl2, 0.7 mmol MPA and 80 mL ddw were mixed into a three-necked flask and pH was adjusted to 11 at room temperature (Figure S1. (b)). Secondly, for a NaHSe solution, 0.4 mmol Se and 1 mmol NaBH4 were mixed in a separate flask. The solution was left under vacuum for 10 min before turning into a nitrogen atmosphere and adding 5 mL ddw. Next, the solution was stirred for 20 min at 80 °C wherein the color was changed from black to transparent and then cooled to room temperature (Figure S1. (a)). Finally, the NaHTe solution is prepared by mixing 0.4 mmol Te, 1 mmol NaBH4 and 10 mL ddw in a separate flask. Then it was stirred for 20 min at 80 °C until the color changed from colorless to pink-purple and then cooled to room temperature (Figure S1. (c)). For CdSeTe QDs, firstly, 0.5 mL NaHSe is added into Cd+2 precursor and then it is stirred for 20 min at 100 °C before adding 0.5 mL of NaHTe solution. At this temperature, CdSeTe QDs grow for about 2 h (Figure S1. (d)). The chemical reactions for the formation of NaHSe and NaHTe are given in equations (2.1) and (2.2), respectively.
(2.1) 4NaBH4 + 2Se + 7H2O→2NaHSe + Na2B4O7 + 14H2
(2.2) 4NaBH4 + 2Te + 7H2O→2NaHTe + Na2B4O7 + 14H2
Finally, the QD solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min with ethanol and dried for further studies.

Firstly, a CdSeTe QDs (7.7 µM) solution was prepared using ddw water as solvent. Then, 10 different solutions for each metabolite in ddw were prepared. The metabolite concentration ranges were adjusted as follows 0.27 µg/mL-8.3 mg/mL for VitC, 0.35 pg/mL-0.35 mg/mL for FA and 0.0312 mg/mL- 16 mg/mL for Glu. To experiment, initially, 2.5 mL QD solution was added to 0.5 mL extra pure water and it was labelled as blank. Then, 2.5 mL QD solution and 0.5 mL metabolite solution are mixed and the fluorescence measurement is recorded. The latter procedure is applied to all solutions of VitC and FA metabolites. For the glucose solutions, 0.25 mL glucose oxidase is added to each of the 0.25 mL glucose solutions and mixed for 10 min to release hydrogen peroxide. Then, the above procedure was applied to all glucose solutions. Fluorescence intensity responses were measured at 380 nm excitation and recorded in the wavelength range of 450–750 nm.

Blood samples were supplied by healthy volunteers from the Düzce University Health Application and Research Center. The informed consent of all human participants was acquired before any studies were conducted, and all procedures were carried out following institutional policies and the applicable laws. The obtained blood samples are stored at −20 °C before use. To remove blood cells, clots or any other complexes from the blood, they are centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm, which is a regular method used in the literature [92]. After the centrifugation process, if still there are traces of unknown compounds in blood serum, they may create a matrix effect on spectroscopic signals. To mitigate this effect effectively, the standard addition method was applied for VitC, FA and glucose detection studies in blood serum samples [93].

Absorption and photoluminescence (PL) results of the QDs were carried out between wavelengths of 350–750 nm. These analyses provide detailed information about the band gap of the materials and their suitability for metabolite detection. The wavelength difference between the emission and absorbance spectra of QDs is defined as the Stokes shift [94]. A Stokes shift of less than 50 nm is observed in organic dyes and fluorescent proteins which inhibits the fluorescence emission due to reabsorption mechanism [95]. In this mechanism, the emitted photons by the fluorophores are self-absorbed by the fluorophores and hence the detection sensitivity is decreased. However, in a material with a large Stoke shift the absorption and PL spectra overlap is minimized, efficiently modifying reabsorption issues and increasing the sensitivity [95]. It is expected that QDs have a large Stokes shift which is especially crucial in biomedical applications as a large Stokes shift reduces self-quenching of fluorophores and improves the signal-to-noise ratio [96].

Fig. 1 (a) shows the absorption and emission graphs of CdSeTe QDs. It is seen that QDs show a distinct excitonic feature and sharp photoluminescence peak at 480 nm and 565 nm, respectively. The peak of clear excitonic feature in the absorption spectra is considered to be the QDs bandgap which is about 2.58 eV (480 nm). A large Stokes shift of 85 nm makes CdSeTe QDs an ideal material to detect metabolites. The emission ranges of CdSeTe QDs studied here are spectrally overlapping with the absorption spectra of VitC, FA and glucose and hence energy transfer from QDs to the metabolites will be efficient, resulting in an effective detection process.

Fig. 1 (b) and (c) show the size distributions of HRTEM images of CdSeTe QDs. After analyzing over 100 particles, the mean size of the QDs was found to be 1.8 nm. The results also show that CdSeTe QDs are spherical in shape and highly crystalline and are well-matched with those reported in the literature [90]. Fig. 1 (d) shows the XRD graphs of the powder CdSeTe QDs. It is seen from the graphs that XRD patterns consist of (111), (220) and (311) planes which match the characteristic peaks of zinc blend structure [86,97–100]. XRD patterns of QDs show that they have a highly cubic crystalline structure. As a result of FTIR analyses performed between 500–4500 nm wavelength, a graph containing the wavenumber-% transmittance information is given in Figure S2 (a) and explains detailed functional groups and bonds.

Fluorescence lifetime is generally used to determine the interaction mechanism of QDs and metabolites [101]. A relatively long lifetime of QDs is desirable for simplifying effective energy transfer between QDs and metabolites. Also, it is critical for improving the sensitivity of the signal-to-noise ratio required for detecting low concentrations of metabolites. The excitation wavelength was used as 377 nm to measure fluorescence lifetimes. The obtained graph for QDs is given in Figure S2 (b) and the radiative component of the lifetime of CdSeTe QDs is calculated as 11.33 ns. Overall, Fig. 1a-d and Figures S1&2 confirm that the synthesized QDs are good quality and suitable for use in metabolite detection. In addition, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the synthesized CdSeTe QDs in the study was determined using the comparative PLQY method, and Rhodamine B was used as the reference fluorescence material [102]. As a result of the analysis, it was determined that the PLQY values ​​of the synthesized QDs were 12.1 %. Although this PLQY value is considerably lower than the PLQY values of the QDs obtained by organometallic synthesis (anhydrous), it is an average value for more water-based CdSeTe QDs.

Understanding the factors that affect the optical behaviour of QDs is crucial for reliability and effectiveness in metabolite detection applications. Before fluorescence measurements, the effects of pH, QD concentration and incubation time were investigated and optimized conditions were used for metabolite detection. Firstly, pH optimization was carried out by changing the pH values of the QD solution from 2 to 12 without changing the QD concentration.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the pH variation considerably affects the CdSeTe QDs’ fluorescence intensity. The surface potential of colloidal nanoparticles was tuned when pH levels changed from 2 to 12 by the dissociation reaction of thiol functional groups (−SH) [49]. The deprotonated thiol (−S) and carboxylate (–COO) were protonated when the pH of the solution was reduced.Consequently, the ligands have been removed from the surface of colloidal nanoparticles as a result of the dissociation reaction in the solution [55]. The fluorescence intensity is quenched at pH values below 6.0. The primary mechanism behind this phenomenon is attributed to the carboxylic groups of MPA existing at the QDs' surface. At pH levels above 6, these groups become deprotonated, prompting a negative surface charge on the QDs. This negative charge improves the solution stability of the nanocrystals by encouraging electrostatic repulsion between them. In opposition, at lower pH levels, protonation of the capping ligands occurs, resulting in neutralization of the surface charge and subsequent aggregation of the nanocrystals [103]. In the pH range 6.0 and 10.0, maximum fluorescence intensity is obtained and hence a pH value of 8 was chosen throughout the study.

QD concentration optimization is carried out to detect the metabolites with the highest sensitivity and rapid responses in a wide linear range [85]. As QD concentration decreases, the fluorescence intensity and thus the linear range of metabolite detection region reduce, however, as QD concentration increases their value surges [104]. To determine the optimum QD concentration, for each metabolite five QD solutions with different molarities were prepared and the fluorescence spectrum of blank and metabolite added were compared. The optimum QD concentration is obtained when (I0-I)/I0 is the highest (I0: fluorescence intensities of the QD solution in the absence of metabolites, I: fluorescence intensities of the QD solution in the presence of metabolites) [81]. Figs. 3, 4 and 5
compare the fluorescence intensity of blank (b) and metabolite-added (a) solutions at different concentrations for VitC, FA and Glu, respectively. Table 1 also shows the (I0-I)/I0 values of each metabolite for the different QD concentrations. All three metabolites exhibit the highest values of (I0-I)/I0 at the highest concentration of CdSeTe QDs which is 7.7 µM. Consequently, 7.7 µM QD concentration is selected as the optimal condition, and all subsequent experiments were carried out using this solution.

To determine the optimized incubation time, 4 different samples each have different metabolite concentration for three types of metabolites were prepared. The fluorescence measurements are recorded at every 2 min for 20 min as shown in Fig. 6 . The optimum incubation time is when the fluorescence responses of QDs stabilized and is found to be as 10 min for VitC, 18 min for FA and 16 min for Glu. C0, F0, and G0 denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence of Vitc, FA and Glu, respectively. Meanwhile, numbers ranging from 1 to 10 indicate the progressively increasing amounts of added metabolites for each solution.

Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity responses of CdSeTe QDs with different concentrations of VitC, FA and Glu are shown in Fig. 7 . It is clearly seen that as the concentration of metabolites increases, the fluorescence intensities decrease. By plotting I0-I against the logarithmic concentration of metabolites graphics, a linear plot is obtained and limit of detection (LOD) of metabolites was calculated according to Eq. (1) where σ is the standard deviation and S is the slope of the linear plot [105,106].

The linear ranges and LODs are 0.3–1600 µg /mL and 1.15 µg/mL for VitC, 3.5E-6–350 µg/mL and 0.84 µg/mL FA and 0.35–90 mM 0.33 mM Glu. Linear ranges, R2, regression equations and LOD values are given in Table 2 . So, this study offers an adequate sensitivity for determination of VitC, FA and Glu in real serum samples.

The proposed method also provides great linear ranges and LOD values for the studied metabolites compared to previous works as shown in Table 3 . Multiple studies calculated variety of LOD values depending on the structure of QDs, size, ligands types (MPA TGA, glutathione, etc.), optimization parameters (pH, temperature, incubation time, etc.), selected linear ranges, solutions, QDs concentration and obtained fluorescence intensity [107–111], as seen in Table 3. In this study, LOD values obtained for VitC, FA and Glu are within the range of the values reported in the literature. It is also found that CdSeTe QDs detect VitC, FA and Glu in wide linear range. In this framework, it is possible to say that CdSeTe QDs which are tested for the first time in the literature to the best of the Author’s knowledge have competitive results and are in good agreement with the previous studies. In addition, each metabolite has a unique detection range and LOD values, so the coexistence of the three metabolites is easily discernible from each other. This is consistent with the literature values wherein more than one metabolite is studied using the same photoluminescence sensor method [28,111,112,113]” These results have successfully shown that CdSeTe QDs can be used as an alternative to other QDs that have been frequently tested by the previous papers and have the potential to give competitive results with other QDs.

The electron–hole pair recombination is the source of quantum dot fluorescence. The effectiveness of this recombination can be influenced by the interactions between molecules on their surface. Numerous interactions, including the inner filter effect, the electron transfer process, and the non-radiative recombination pathway, can be used to explain the quenching of fluorescence intensity [105,142–144]. Particularly, in metabolite detection studies, biological intermolecular interactions with QDs are generally based on FRET process [51,52,86,105,144,145]. According to the FRET mechanism, non-destructive and non-radiative energy transfer occurs depending on the distance between the donor (QDs) and the acceptor (metabolite) [146,147]. Dipole-dipole interactions occurring between QDs and quenching metabolites cause energy transfer without forming a photon in the FRET mechanism. This mechanism was explained by the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the CdSeTe QDs (donors) and the absorption spectra of the metabolites (acceptors) as shown in Figure S3 for FA which is similar with VitC and Glu.

Extractes R0, r0 and r0/R0 values for all types of metabolites are illustrated in Table 4 . Since R0 r0 for all metabolites remain in the range of 20–90 Å and 10–100 Å, respectively. The interaction between CdSeTe QDs and metabolites used in this study is based on FRET mechanism [144,148,150]. In addition, for efficiently for transferring energy in FRET mechanism, r0/R0 should be in the range of 0.5–2 [148], which well agrees with our results. Thus, it can be said that FRET type energy transfer from the excited state of CdSeTe QDs and VitC, FA, and Glu is responsible for the quenching process.

In order to apply CdSeTe QDs for the determination of VitC, FA and Glu in complex matrices like blood, the selectivity studies were accomplished by investigating the fluorescence intensity changes of QD solutions against potential interfering substances. Selectivity studies were completed with two different solutions; one is heavy metal ions (S1- Pb+2, Sb+3, Hg+2, As+3, Cd+2) and the other is metal ions (S2- Ca+2, K+1, Na+1, Mg+2, Fe+2, Cu+2, Co+2, Zn+2, Mn+2, Ni+2, Mo+2, B+3). Blood contains a variety of metal ions, both essential and trace heavy metals, which can be present due to environmental exposure or pathological conditions. These ions can potentially interfere with the fluorescence detection systems. Testing the effects of both essential metal ions and heavy metals helps to evaluate the QDs' selectivity and potential interferences under physiological and pathological conditions. This is critical for developing reliable biosensors for blood-based diagnostics. Additionally, heavy metals are known to cause fluorescence quenching through various mechanisms, such as complex formation and redox interactions. The testing of these ions allows to benchmark the quenching effects against metabolites.

As seen in Fig. 8 , the combination of S1 and S2 mixtures causes some quenching in the emission values ​​of CdSeTe QDs. It is known that ions can cause PL quenching [153]. In contrast, the passivation that occurs at the end surfaces of the MPA ligand used in the synthesis process of CdSeTe QDs is seen as a barrier preventing the contact of ions with the surface and the PL quenching rates are in the border regions. However, VitC, Glu and FA metabolites added separately in the solution caused a much higher PL quenching and competition with the ions.Thus, we can conclude that quenching method managing with CdSeTe QDs has a good selectivity against chosen metabolites in complex environment.

In this study, we also applied CdSeTe alloy QDs based detection method to detect VitC, FA and Glu in real blood serum samples for the first time in the literature. Similar to the previous studies [81,151,152] for the detection system, the standard addition method was carried out and the results are given in Table 5 . In the standard addition method, the content of the environment is unknown or has a matrix effect which affects responses. Because of this, standard solutions were prepared containing components close to the concentration in the blood sample to be used. In this study, detection studies were carried out by adding known amounts of metabolites to blood samples, similar to previous studies [28,118,137,140,132] and it is aimed to detect them with the lowest error. Recovery and linear detection ranges are, respectively, found to be 95.51–104.11 %, 10–20 µg/mL for VitC, 95.05–104.91 %, 7.5–20 ng/mL for FA and 95.30–104.38 %, 0.25–1.5 mg/mL for Glu with the error no more than 5 % in three blood serum samples. In the light of these results, it is successfully proved that the developed detection system with CdSeTe QDs in this study has potential uses in the real-world quantification of metabolites. Thus, this QD can be used as an alternative to other QDs that have been frequently tested before and can compete with other QDs.

In this study, a fluorescent sensor detection system based on CdSeTe synthesized with MPA in aqueous medium was developed for the selective and sensitive detection of VitC, FA and Glu. Linear correlation ranges for VitC, FA and Glu are found to be 0.3–1600 µg/mL, 3.5E-6–350 µg/mL and 0.35–90 mM, respectively. The LOD value for VitC, FA and Glu were also calculated to be 1.15 µg/mL, 0.84 µg/mL and 0.33 mM, respectively. The quenching in the fluorescence intensity of CdSeTe QDs is attributed to the FRET mechanism. This newly developed system also offers a rapid, sensitive and selective detection method for VitC, FA and Glu in real blood serum samples. Thus, metabolic disorders related to these metabolites can be potentially detected by CdSeTe QDs based systems. In general, the upcoming scenarios of CdSeTe QDs in the biomedical field are promising. With continuous improvements, CdSeTe QDs show positive visions to advance bio-sensing, diagnostics, imaging, drug delivery and any other biomedical applications in the next ten years.
